title: Zechariah 10
subtitle: The LORD Will Care for His Flock and Restore His People

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — "My Anger Burns Against the Shepherds"

heb.paragraph: The terafim were small household idols used for divination and domestic cult (Gen 31:19, 34-35; Judg 17:5; 18:14-20; 1 Sam 19:13; Ezek 21:21; Hos 3:4). Their mention in Zechariah 10:2 is striking: after the exile, idolatry was supposedly eradicated from Israel. Zechariah's reference may be retrospective (the ancestors' idolatry caused the exile), or it may indicate that some forms of folk religion persisted in the post-exilic community.

The 'cornerstone' (pinnah, literally 'corner') in 10:4 is a royal metaphor: 'From Judah will come the cornerstone.' The cornerstone anchors and aligns the entire building (cf. Isa 28:16; Ps 118:22). Applied to a leader from Judah, it signifies the stable, foundational ruler who holds the community together. The NT applies this imagery to Christ (Matt 21:42; Eph 2:20; 1 Pet 2:6-7).

panels.ctx: Chapter 10 continues the restoration themes of chapter 9 but shifts focus from the messianic king to the flock-shepherd relationship. The chapter opens with a call to ask the LORD — not idols or diviners — for rain (10:1-2), then transitions to God's anger against the 'shepherds' (leaders) who have failed the flock (10:3). The metaphor shifts from negative (bad shepherds) to positive: from Judah will come four instruments of leadership — the cornerstone, the tent peg, the battle bow, and 'every ruler' (10:4). These images describe a self-sufficient leadership structure that produces its own governors rather than depending on foreign overlords. The people themselves will become 'like warriors in battle' (10:5), empowered by God's presence.

cross.note: Ezekiel's extended indictment of Israel's shepherds who exploit and scatter the flock provides the primary background for Zechariah's shepherd critique.
Isaiah's 'precious cornerstone' in Zion — tested, sure, foundational — develops the same architectural metaphor for stable leadership that Zechariah uses in 10:4.
Jeremiah's accusation that 'the shepherds are senseless and do not inquire of the LORD' parallels Zechariah's contrast between divine provision (10:1) and false divination (10:2).

notes.note: The contrast between asking the LORD for rain and consulting idols/diviners is a fundamental test of covenant loyalty. Rain in ancient Israel was understood as a direct divine gift (Deut 11:14; 28:12), and seeking it from idols was covenant treason. The people wander 'like sheep' because they lack a true shepherd — a leadership vacuum that produces theological confusion.

God's anger against the 'shepherds' (political and religious leaders) and His decision to 'care for his flock' establishes the divine shepherd theme that will dominate chapters 10-11. When human leadership fails, God Himself intervenes as the good shepherd.

The four leadership images — cornerstone, tent peg, battle bow, ruler — all emerge 'from Judah,' affirming the Davidic tribe as the source of legitimate governance. The tent peg (yated) holds the tent together (cf. Isa 22:23-24); the battle bow secures military defense. Together they describe a complete leadership apparatus.

Calvin saw in the contrast between true and false guidance (LORD vs. idols) the perpetual danger facing God's people: substituting human contrivances for divine provision. The shepherds' failure is not merely incompetence but infidelity — they led the flock to idols instead of to God. The fourfold leadership from Judah demonstrates that God's remedy is structural: He raises up righteous leaders, not merely righteous individuals.

The NET notes observe that the word qosemim ('diviners') refers to practitioners of divination — a practice explicitly condemned in Deuteronomy 18:10-14. Their 'false dreams' and 'vain comfort' stand in direct contrast to the true prophetic word through Zechariah.

The four images from Judah may refer to four functions of leadership (foundation, stability, defense, governance) or to four specific leaders. The NET notes prefer the functional reading, understanding these as metaphors for the comprehensive leadership that God will raise from the Davidic tribe.

Boda reads this section as the 'shepherd transition oracle,' which moves from failed human leadership (shepherds who do not care) to divinely appointed Judahite leadership (cornerstone, tent peg, battle bow, ruler). The critique of terafim and diviners (10:2) is surprising in a post-exilic context and may reflect Zechariah's awareness that popular religion had not been fully purified by the exile. The fourfold leadership from Judah (10:4) responds to the leadership vacuum by promising not a single messianic figure but a comprehensive governing apparatus rooted in the Davidic tribe.

sections.header: Verses 6–12 — "I Will Strengthen Judah and Save the Tribes of Joseph"

heb.paragraph: The verb sharaq ('to whistle, hiss') in 10:8 describes God's method of gathering His scattered people. The image is pastoral: a shepherd whistles to summon the flock. But it also recalls the menacing use of the same verb in Isaiah 5:26 and 7:18, where God 'whistles' for the Assyrian and Egyptian armies as instruments of judgment. In Zechariah, the whistle that once summoned punishers now summons the redeemed.

panels.ctx: The second half of chapter 10 expands the restoration promise to include both Judah (the southern kingdom) and 'the tribes of Joseph' (the northern kingdom, Ephraim). This is remarkable: the northern kingdom had been destroyed by Assyria in 722 BC, and its population scattered among the nations. Zechariah promises their restoration 'as though I had not rejected them' (10:6) — a complete reversal of the judgment of 722 BC. The gathering will extend from Egypt and Assyria (10:10), the two historic oppressors, and will involve a new exodus: 'They will pass through the sea of trouble' (10:11), recalling the Red Sea crossing. The chapter concludes with the promise of divine strengthening: 'I will strengthen them in the LORD and in his name they will live securely' (10:12).

cross.note: Hosea's promise that the rejected northern kingdom will be restored and called 'my people' again parallels Zechariah's inclusion of Ephraim/Joseph in the restoration.
Isaiah's vision of a 'second exodus' gathering Israel from Assyria, Egypt, and the nations provides the template for Zechariah's new exodus imagery in 10:10-11.
Ezekiel's vision of the two sticks (Judah and Ephraim) joined into one parallels Zechariah's unified restoration of Judah and the tribes of Joseph.

notes.note: The inclusion of 'the tribes of Joseph' (Ephraim) in the restoration promise is theologically profound. The northern kingdom's destruction in 722 BC appeared to be God's final word on their covenant unfaithfulness. But Zechariah declares that God's compassion overrides the finality of that judgment: He will restore them 'as though I had not rejected them.'

God's 'whistling' for His people combines pastoral tenderness with sovereign authority. The scattered diaspora community, spread among nations where they 'remember me,' will respond to God's summons and 'return' — the same shuv that opened the book (1:3).

The new exodus from Egypt and Assyria deliberately evokes the original exodus and the Assyrian captivity. Both empires are now symbols of oppression from which God liberates His people. The 'sea of trouble' through which they pass recalls the Red Sea — God's sovereignty over water remains the paradigmatic image of salvation.

The concluding promise — 'I will strengthen them in the LORD' — grounds the entire restoration in divine empowerment, not human initiative. The people will 'live securely' (walk, halakh) 'in his name,' meaning their entire way of life will be defined by God's character and authority.

Calvin found in the restoration of Ephraim a powerful illustration of divine grace: God restores even those who were most severely judged, not because they deserved it but because His compassion is boundless. The new exodus imagery demonstrates that God's saving power is not a past memory but a present and future reality, repeatable in every generation of the church.

The pairing of Egypt and Assyria as the lands of return represents the full scope of exile: Assyria deported the northern kingdom (722 BC) and Egypt had been a place of flight and refuge (cf. Jer 43-44). The mention of Gilead and Lebanon as destinations indicates the full territorial extent of restoration.

The 'sea of trouble' (yam tsarah) is textually difficult; some manuscripts read 'the sea, Egypt' (yam mitsrayim). The NET notes retain the MT, understanding 'trouble' as a descriptor of the Red Sea experience: the sea that brought trouble to their enemies.

Boda reads the chapter's second half as a 'new exodus oracle' that extends the restoration beyond Judah to include the lost northern tribes. This pan-Israelite scope is significant: Zechariah is not merely promising the rebuilding of the post-exilic community in Jerusalem but the eschatological reunification of all twelve tribes. The exodus typology (10:10-11) creates continuity between God's foundational saving act and His future redemption, while the whistling/gathering imagery (10:8) casts God in the role of a shepherd recollecting a scattered flock.

themes.note: Chapter 10 is dominated by Mercy (God's compassionate restoration including Ephraim), Sovereignty (the divine shepherd who gathers and strengthens), Covenant (the pan-Israelite scope of restoration), and Judgment (against false shepherds and idolatrous diviners).

rows.label: vv.1-5
rows.text: Ask the LORD, not idols: divine anger at failed shepherds and the promise of fourfold leadership from Judah

rows.label: vv.6-12
rows.text: Restoration of Judah and Joseph: new exodus from Egypt and Assyria, divine strengthening

lit.note: Chapter 10 develops the shepherd-flock theology, moving from the failure of human leadership to God's direct care, and extending the restoration promise to include the lost northern tribes in a new exodus.

hebtext.note: The terafim were small household idols used for divination and domestic cult (Gen 31:19, 34-35; Judg 17:5; 18:14-20; 1 Sam 19:13; Ezek 21:21; Hos 3:4). ppl.name: Zechariah
ppl.text: Their 'false dreams' and 'vain comfort' stand in direct contrast to the true prophetic word through Zechariah…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The LORD Will Care for His Flock and Restore His People

translations.text: “I will strengthen Judah and save the tribes of Joseph. I will restore them because I have compassion on them. They will be as though I had not rejected them, for I am the Lord their God and I will answer them.

“I will strengthen the house of Judah, and I will save the house of Joseph. I will bring them back because I have compassion on them, and they shall be as though I had not rejected them, for I am the LORD their God and I will answer them.
From Judah will come the cornerstone, from him the tent peg, from him the battle bow, from him every ruler.
From him shall come the cornerstone, from him the tent peg, from him the battle bow, from him every ruler—all of them together.
